worth_it_explanation: Viewability measurement is the foundation of every performance and efficiency claim a campaign makes. Without a clear standard applied consistently, the viewable rate reported in DoubleVerify (DV) Pinnacle may reflect only the impressions the tag happened to measure, not the full delivered universe. Each check in this section is a configuration and methodology audit: confirming the measurement standard in use, confirming that the measured rate is factored into the viewable rate rather than ignored, and confirming that a viewability goal, when set, is acted on when performance falls short.
intro: The Media Rating Council (MRC) defines a viewable display impression as one where at least 50% of the ad's pixels are in view for a minimum of one continuous second; for video, the threshold is 50% of pixels in view for at least two continuous seconds. DoubleVerify measures against this MRC standard by default and also offers stricter custom thresholds, such as requiring the ad to be fully on screen or to remain in view for a longer duration. These checks confirm that the standard in use is understood and documented, that the measured rate is correctly accounted for when reading viewable rate figures, and that viewability goals have teeth when campaigns fall short of them.

audit_checks.title: The viewability standard applied in DoubleVerify is not documented or confirmed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the viewability standard in use is not documented, different stakeholders interpret the viewable rate against different benchmarks. A campaign that meets a custom threshold but fails the MRC floor, or vice versa, can produce conflicting conclusions depending on which standard the reader assumed. The ambiguity compounds when results are shared with clients or used to set future targets.

educate.base:
DoubleVerify (DV) measures viewability against the MRC standard by default: at least 50% of the ad's pixels must be in view for a minimum of one continuous second for display ads, and for a minimum of two continuous seconds for video ads. DV Pinnacle also supports stricter custom thresholds, such as requiring 100% of pixels in view (fully on-screen) or a longer continuous in-view duration, which DV refers to as custom viewability metrics.

The standard applied to a given campaign or line item determines what counts as a viewable impression in the DV reporting dashboard. If the account uses a custom threshold and a stakeholder reads the viewable rate against the MRC floor, the reported percentage will look stronger than it actually is under the stricter measure. The reverse is also true: a campaign measured against the MRC floor may be benchmarked against a stricter internal standard by a client expecting a different definition.

A few signs suggest the standard has not been confirmed:
  • The DV Pinnacle dashboard shows viewability data, but no documentation exists for which threshold is applied at the campaign or advertiser level
  • Campaign briefs or media plans reference a viewability goal without specifying whether it is the MRC standard or a custom threshold
  • Different line items in the same campaign use different viewability definitions without a documented rationale
You may want to confirm the measurement standard directly in DV Pinnacle under the campaign or advertiser settings before comparing viewability figures across campaigns or against external benchmarks. The MRC accreditation scope for DV's viewability reporting is documented on the MRC digital accreditation page.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running display and video across multiple placements, a custom viewability threshold applied only to video while display defaults to the MRC floor can produce a viewable rate figure that blends two different standards in the same report. You may want to confirm the threshold per format before drawing conclusions from aggregate viewability data.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser where viewability is used as a proxy for brand exposure, the difference between the MRC floor and a fully-on-screen custom threshold can be significant. A campaign that meets a 50% in-view threshold may still have a large share of impressions where the ad was only partially visible. You may want to confirm which standard your client expects before presenting viewability results.

investigate.intro: Confirm, for each active campaign, which viewability threshold is applied in DV Pinnacle, and verify that the same standard is reflected in any campaign brief, media plan, or client-facing report referencing viewability figures.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the Quality dashboard or the relevant advertiser account.

Open the campaign or line-item view and locate the viewability reporting section. Confirm whether the viewable rate shown reflects the MRC standard (50% of pixels in view for 1 continuous second for display, 2 continuous seconds for video) or a custom threshold configured for this advertiser.
In the account or campaign settings, check whether a custom viewability definition has been applied. DV Pinnacle surfaces this as a custom metric setting at the advertiser or campaign level.
Document the threshold in use for each active campaign. Note any campaigns where the threshold differs from the account default.

Compare the documented threshold against any viewability goals cited in the campaign brief or client agreement. Flag mismatches where the goal references a standard different from the one being measured.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the viewability threshold in use for each active campaign and whether it matches the standard referenced in the campaign brief or client agreement. A campaign where the applied threshold is documented and matches the referenced standard passes. A campaign where no threshold is documented, or where the threshold does not match the standard cited in the brief, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the nature of the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the viewability threshold in use and the standard referenced in the campaign brief for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the viewability standard applied in DoubleVerify for {{site_name}}'s campaigns is not documented or confirmed against the standard referenced in campaign briefs and client-facing reports. When the measurement threshold in use differs from the one stakeholders assume, viewable rate figures produce conflicting interpretations: a campaign can appear to meet or miss a goal depending entirely on which definition the reader applied. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the viewability threshold setting in DV Pinnacle for each active campaign, documenting the standard in the campaign brief, and aligning the measurement definition with any viewability goals stated in client agreements before presenting results.

audit_checks.title: Viewable rate is read without accounting for the measured rate, overstating true viewability
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A viewable rate calculated only against measured impressions can be significantly higher than the true viewable rate across all delivered impressions. When the measured rate is low, a large share of the delivered universe carries no viewability signal. Reading only the viewable rate of measured impressions treats the unmeasured portion as if it does not exist, producing an overstated picture of campaign viewability that feeds inaccurate benchmarks and performance assessments.

educate.base:
DV Pinnacle reports two related but distinct figures: the measured rate and the viewable rate. The measured rate is the share of delivered impressions that the DV tag successfully evaluated for viewability. The viewable rate is the share of measured impressions that met the viewability threshold. These two figures combine to determine the true viewable rate across all delivered impressions.

A viewable rate of 70% sounds strong in isolation, but if the measured rate is 60%, it means DV evaluated only 60% of delivered impressions. Of those, 70% were viewable. The remaining 40% of delivered impressions carry no viewability signal at all. The true viewable rate across all delivered impressions is 0.70 multiplied by 0.60, or 42%, not 70%.

DV Pinnacle surfaces both figures in the Quality dashboard. The problem arises when reporting focuses on the viewable rate of measured impressions without surfacing the measured rate alongside it. A few situations reduce the measured rate and create this gap:
  • Creatives served in environments where the DV tag cannot load, such as certain mobile in-app environments without Open Measurement SDK (OM SDK) integration
  • Ad placements where the creative loads but the DV tag fires after a page abandonment
  • Campaigns running across supply that has limited third-party tag support
You may want to pull both figures from DV Pinnacle for each campaign and compute the effective viewable rate across total delivered impressions before presenting results or setting future targets. The IAB Tech Lab's Open Measurement SDK addresses measurement coverage gaps in mobile in-app and connected TV environments.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running across a mix of open-web display and mobile in-app inventory, the measured rate for in-app placements may be meaningfully lower than for open-web placements if the app supply does not support OM SDK integration. You may want to check the measured rate by environment type, not only at the campaign level, to understand where the coverage gaps sit.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser reporting viewability to a client as part of a brand-awareness or reach guarantee, a viewable rate figure presented without the corresponding measured rate may overstate the guarantee's fulfillment. You may want to confirm that client-facing reports include the measured rate alongside the viewable rate so the effective viewable impression count is transparent.

investigate.intro: Pull both the measured rate and the viewable rate from DV Pinnacle for each active campaign, compute the effective viewable rate across total delivered impressions, and compare it against any viewability goals in the campaign brief.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the Quality dashboard for the relevant advertiser.
For each active campaign, locate the Viewability section. Note both the 'Viewable Rate' figure and the 'Measured Rate' figure. Both should be visible in the campaign-level Quality reporting.

Compute the effective viewable rate: multiply the Viewable Rate by the Measured Rate. For example, a Viewable Rate of 70% and a Measured Rate of 60% gives an effective viewable rate of 42% across all delivered impressions.

Compare the effective viewable rate against any viewability goal cited in the campaign brief or client agreement. If the brief references only a viewable rate without specifying whether it is computed against measured or total impressions, note the ambiguity.

If the measured rate is below 80%, identify which placements or environments are contributing to the unmeasured share. DV Pinnacle allows segmentation by environment (desktop, mobile web, mobile in-app, connected TV) to isolate where coverage gaps occur.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the measured rate, the viewable rate of measured impressions, and the computed effective viewable rate across total delivered impressions. A campaign where the measured rate is 80% or above and the effective viewable rate meets the campaign goal passes. A campaign where the measured rate is below 80%, or where the effective viewable rate falls materially below the campaign goal when computed correctly, is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the measured rate, the viewable rate, and the effective viewable rate.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measured rate and viewable rate for each campaign above to compute the effective viewable rate and see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that viewability reporting for {{site_name}}'s campaigns in DoubleVerify presents the viewable rate without surfacing the measured rate alongside it, which overstates the true viewability picture across all delivered impressions. A viewable rate calculated only against the impressions DV successfully evaluated excludes the unmeasured portion of delivery entirely. When the measured rate is below 80%, the effective viewable rate across total delivered impressions can be substantially lower than the reported figure suggests. {{voice_recommend}} pulling both the measured rate and the viewable rate from DV Pinnacle for each active campaign, computing the effective viewable rate by multiplying the two figures, and including both metrics in any client-facing report or internal performance review that references a viewability figure.

audit_checks.title: A viewability goal is set in DoubleVerify but underperformance is not acted on
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A viewability goal that triggers no action when missed functions as a reporting label rather than a performance control. Supply that consistently delivers below the goal continues to receive spend, the goal's stated value is not realized, and the data accumulated against it cannot be used to hold supply accountable or negotiate make-goods. The gap between the goal and the outcome is documented in DV Pinnacle but not closed.

educate.base:
Setting a viewability goal in DV Pinnacle or in a buying platform creates a benchmark: the campaign should deliver impressions at or above a specified viewable rate. DV records whether delivery meets that benchmark, but the measurement system does not automatically remove supply that falls short. Action on underperformance requires a process that connects the DV data to a decision: blocking the underperforming placement, raising the viewability threshold in the buying platform's targeting, escalating to the publisher or supply-side platform for a make-good, or adjusting budget allocation away from low-viewability inventory.

Without that process, a goal in DV Pinnacle is a passive record. The DV dashboard may show that 30% of placements are delivering below the stated goal, but if the account does not have a defined response, the placements continue to receive budget in the next flight.

A few signs suggest the goal is not being enforced:
  • DV Pinnacle shows a viewability goal configured at the campaign or advertiser level, but the same placements appear below the goal across multiple consecutive reporting periods
  • There is no documented process for reviewing DV viewability alerts or for escalating placements that miss the goal
  • Buying platform targeting does not use DV's viewability pre-bid segment to prevent low-viewability impressions from entering the auction
You may want to define a response protocol before the next campaign flight: a threshold below which a placement is blocked, a timeline for requesting a publisher make-good, and a confirmation step that the buying platform's targeting settings reflect the same viewability standard as the DV goal.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser where viewable impressions are tied to a brand-awareness or reach metric, consistently allowing placements to run below the viewability goal dilutes the efficiency of every impression bought. You may want to review whether the buying platform's viewability pre-bid segment is set to the same threshold as the DV goal so that supply falling below the threshold is blocked before the bid rather than flagged after delivery.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser billing by viewable impression or using viewability as a client-facing guarantee, underperformance against a stated goal creates a contractual exposure. You may want to confirm whether the campaign's media agreement includes a viewability guarantee and, if so, whether the process for requesting make-goods from supply partners is documented.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a viewability goal is configured in DV Pinnacle, and if so, whether the account has a defined process for reviewing and acting on placements that fall below that goal across multiple consecutive reporting periods.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the Quality dashboard for the relevant advertiser.
Confirm whether a viewability goal is set at the campaign or advertiser level. Note the threshold configured (e.g., 70% viewable rate).
Pull a viewability report segmented by placement or site for the current flight. Identify placements that have delivered below the goal threshold across two or more consecutive reporting periods.

Review whether any action has been taken on those placements: a block applied in the buying platform, an escalation to the publisher, or a targeting adjustment. If no action is documented, note the placement names and the number of periods they have run below goal.

Check the buying platform (e.g., the DSP used for this campaign) to confirm whether DV's viewability pre-bid segment is applied as a targeting inclusion, which would prevent low-viewability impressions from entering the auction before delivery.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a viewability goal is configured and, for each placement running below that goal for two or more consecutive reporting periods, whether a documented response exists. A campaign where placements below the goal have been blocked, escalated, or adjusted within one reporting period passes. A campaign where placements have run below the goal for two or more periods with no documented response is a fail, recorded with the campaign name, the placement names, the number of periods below goal, and the gap between the placement's viewable rate and the stated goal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record any placements running below the viewability goal and whether a response was documented for each above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that a viewability goal is configured in DoubleVerify for {{site_name}}'s campaigns but that placements consistently delivering below the goal have not triggered a documented response. A viewability goal without an enforcement process is a reporting label: DV records the gap between delivery and the target, but supply that misses the goal continues to receive budget across subsequent flights. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a response protocol that defines the threshold below which a placement is blocked or escalated, the timeline for requesting a make-good from supply partners, and a confirmation step that the buying platform's viewability pre-bid segment is aligned with the DV goal threshold so that low-viewability supply is excluded before the bid rather than flagged after delivery.
